question: B-6. As usual, let \( \sigma(N) \) denote the sum of all the (positive integral) divisors of \( N \). (Included among these divisors are \( I \) and \( N \) itself.) For example, if \( p \) is a prime, then \( \sigma(p)=p+1 \). Motivated by the notion of a "perfect" number, a positive integer \( N \) is called "quasiperfect" if \( \sigma(N)=2 N+1 \). Prove that every quasiperfect number is the square of an odd integer.
solution:
B-6.
Let \( N=2^{\alpha} p_{1}^{\beta_{1}} p_{2}^{\beta_{2}} \cdots p_{k}^{\beta_{k}} \) where \( \alpha \) and the \( \beta_{1} \) are nonnegative integers and the \( p_{1} \) are distinct odd primes. Then
\[
\sigma(N)=\sigma\left(2^{\alpha}\right) \sigma\left(p_{1}^{\beta_{1}}\right) \cdots \sigma\left(p_{k}^{\beta_{k}}\right) .
\]

Since \( \sigma(N)=2 N+1 \) is odd, it follows that \( \sigma\left(p_{i}^{\beta_{1}}\right) \) is odd, \( 1 \leqq i \leqq k \). But
\[
\sigma\left(p_{i}^{\beta_{i}}\right)=1+p_{i}+p_{i}^{2}+\cdots+p_{i}^{\beta_{1}}
\]
is odd if and only if \( \beta_{1} \) is even; for if \( \beta_{1} \) were odd, the right hand side would be the sum of an even number of odd numbers and hence even. It follows that the odd part of \( N \) must be a square, so that we may write
\[
N=2^{\alpha} M^{2}, \alpha \geqq 0 .
\]
where \( M \) is odd. It remains to show that \( \alpha=0 \).
Since \( N \) is quasiperfect, \( \sigma(N)=2^{\alpha+1} M^{2}+1 \), while from (1) we deduce \( \sigma(N)=\sigma\left(2^{\alpha}\right) \sigma\left(M^{2}\right)= \) \( \left(2^{\alpha+1}-1\right) \sigma\left(M^{2}\right) \). Hence \( 2^{\alpha+1} M^{2}+1=\left(2^{\alpha+1}-1\right) \sigma\left(M^{2}\right) \) so that
\[
M^{2}+1 \equiv 0\left(\bmod 2^{\alpha+1}-1\right)
\]

If \( \alpha>0,2^{\alpha+1}-1 \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \). Consequently \( 2^{\alpha+1}-1 \) has a prime divisor \( p \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \). Equation (2) implies
\[
M^{2}+1 \equiv 0 \quad(\bmod p)
\]

But since -1 is a quadratic non-residue modulo \( p \) whenever \( p \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \), (3) is impossible. Thus. \( \alpha=0 \).

descriptive_long.question: B-6. As usual, let \( divisorsum(quasint) \) denote the sum of all the (positive integral) divisors of \( quasint \). (Included among these divisors are \( I \) and \( quasint \) itself.) For example, if \( primevar \) is a prime, then \( divisorsum(primevar)=primevar+1 \). Motivated by the notion of a "perfect" number, a positive integer \( quasint \) is called "quasiperfect" if \( divisorsum(quasint)=2 quasint+1 \). Prove that every quasiperfect number is the square of an odd integer.
descriptive_long.solution:
B-6.
Let \( quasint=2^{alphaexpt}\,primeone^{betaoneex}\,primetwo^{\beta_{2}} \cdots primekth^{\beta_{k}} \) where \( alphaexpt \) and \( betaoneex \) are nonnegative integers and the \( primeone \) are distinct odd primes. Then
\[
divisorsum(quasint)=divisorsum\left(2^{alphaexpt}\right)\,divisorsum\left(primeone^{betaoneex}\right)\cdots divisorsum\left(primekth^{\beta_{k}}\right).
\]
Since \( divisorsum(quasint)=2\,quasint+1 \) is odd, it follows that \( divisorsum\left(primeith^{betaithex}\right) \) is odd, \( 1\leqq indexvar\leqq primcount \). But
\[
divisorsum\left(primeith^{betaithex}\right)=1+primeith+primeith^{2}+\cdots+primeith^{betaithex}
\]
is odd if and only if \( betaithex \) is even; for if \( betaithex \) were odd, the right-hand side would be the sum of an even number of odd summands and hence even. Therefore the odd part of \( quasint \) must be a square, so we may write
\[
quasint=2^{alphaexpt}\,oddroot^{2},\qquad alphaexpt\geqq 0,
\]
with \( oddroot \) odd. It remains to prove that \( alphaexpt=0 \).

Because \( quasint \) is quasiperfect, \( divisorsum(quasint)=2^{alphaexpt+1}oddroot^{2}+1 \); on the other hand, from (1) we obtain
\[
divisorsum(quasint)=divisorsum\left(2^{alphaexpt}\right)\,divisorsum\left(oddroot^{2}\right)=\left(2^{alphaexpt+1}-1\right)divisorsum\left(oddroot^{2}\right).
\]
Hence
\[
2^{alphaexpt+1}oddroot^{2}+1=\left(2^{alphaexpt+1}-1\right)divisorsum\left(oddroot^{2}\right),
\]
so that
\[
oddroot^{2}+1\equiv 0\pmod{2^{alphaexpt+1}-1}.
\]
If \( alphaexpt>0 \) then \( 2^{alphaexpt+1}-1\equiv 3\pmod{4} \). Consequently \( 2^{alphaexpt+1}-1 \) possesses a prime divisor \( primevar\equiv 3\pmod{4} \). From the congruence above we would have
\[
oddroot^{2}+1\equiv 0\pmod{primevar},
\]
but \(-1\) is a quadratic non-residue modulo any prime congruent to \(3\pmod 4\), a contradiction. Therefore \( alphaexpt=0 \).

Thus every quasiperfect number is the square of an odd integer.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: B-6. As usual, let \( raspberry(marshmallow) \) denote the sum of all the (positive integral) divisors of \( marshmallow \). (Included among these divisors are \( I \) and \( marshmallow \) itself.) For example, if \( lollipop \) is a prime, then \( raspberry(lollipop)=lollipop+1 \). Motivated by the notion of a "perfect" number, a positive integer \( marshmallow \) is called "quasiperfect" if \( raspberry(marshmallow)=2 marshmallow+1 \). Prove that every quasiperfect number is the square of an odd integer.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-6.
Let \( marshmallow=2^{cinnamon} peppermint^{chocolate} butterscotch^{\beta_{2}} \cdots gingerbread^{\beta_{k}} \) where \( cinnamon \) and the \( chocolate \) are nonnegative integers and the \( peppermint \) are distinct odd primes. Then
\[
raspberry(marshmallow)=raspberry\left(2^{cinnamon}\right) raspberry\left(peppermint^{chocolate}\right) \cdots raspberry\left(gingerbread^{\beta_{k}}\right) .
\]

Since \( raspberry(marshmallow)=2 marshmallow+1 \) is odd, it follows that \( raspberry\left(cheesecake^{chocolate}\right) \) is odd, \( 1 \leqq tangerine \leqq shortcake \). But
\[
raspberry\left(cheesecake^{strawberry}\right)=1+cheesecake+cheesecake^{2}+\cdots+cheesecake^{chocolate}
\]
is odd if and only if \( chocolate \) is even; for if \( chocolate \) were odd, the right hand side would be the sum of an even number of odd numbers and hence even. It follows that the odd part of marshmallow must be a square, so that we may write
\[
marshmallow=2^{cinnamon} blueberry^{2}, cinnamon \geqq 0 .
\]
where blueberry is odd. It remains to show that \( cinnamon=0 \).
Since marshmallow is quasiperfect, \( raspberry(marshmallow)=2^{cinnamon+1} blueberry^{2}+1 \), while from (1) we deduce \( raspberry(marshmallow)=raspberry\left(2^{cinnamon}\right) raspberry\left(blueberry^{2}\right)= \left(2^{cinnamon+1}-1\right) raspberry\left(blueberry^{2}\right) \). Hence \( 2^{cinnamon+1} blueberry^{2}+1=\left(2^{cinnamon+1}-1\right) raspberry\left(blueberry^{2}\right) \) so that
\[
blueberry^{2}+1 \equiv 0\left(\bmod 2^{cinnamon+1}-1\right)
\]

If \( cinnamon>0,2^{cinnamon+1}-1 \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \). Consequently \( 2^{cinnamon+1}-1 \) has a prime divisor \( lollipop \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \). Equation (2) implies
\[
blueberry^{2}+1 \equiv 0 \quad(\bmod lollipop)
\]

But since -1 is a quadratic non-residue modulo \( lollipop \) whenever \( lollipop \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \), (3) is impossible. Thus. \( cinnamon=0 \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question: B-6. As usual, let \( divergence(infinitesimal) \) denote the sum of all the (positive integral) divisors of \( infinitesimal \). (Included among these divisors are \( I \) and \( infinitesimal \) itself.) For example, if \( compositefactor \) is a prime, then \( divergence(compositefactor)=compositefactor+1 \). Motivated by the notion of a "perfect" number, a positive integer \( infinitesimal \) is called "quasiperfect" if \( divergence(infinitesimal)=2 infinitesimal+1 \). Prove that every quasiperfect number is the square of an odd integer.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-6.
Let \( infinitesimal=2^{zeropower} compositeone^{unevenexponent} compositetwo^{\beta_{2}} \cdots compositekay^{\beta_{k}} \) where \( zeropower \) and the \( unevenexponent \) are nonnegative integers and the \( compositeone \) are distinct odd primes. Then
\[
divergence(infinitesimal)=divergence\left(2^{zeropower}\right) divergence\left(compositeone^{unevenexponent}\right) \cdots divergence\left(compositekay^{\beta_{k}}\right) .
\]

Since \( divergence(infinitesimal)=2 infinitesimal+1 \) is odd, it follows that \( divergence\left(compositeindex^{\beta_{1}}\right) \) is odd, \( 1 \leqq i \leqq solitude \). But
\[
divergence\left(compositeindex^{\beta_{i}}\right)=1+compositeindex+compositeindex^{2}+\cdots+compositeindex^{\beta_{1}}
\]
is odd if and only if \( \beta_{1} \) is even; for if \( \beta_{1} \) were odd, the right hand side would be the sum of an even number of odd numbers and hence even. It follows that the odd part of \( infinitesimal \) must be a square, so that we may write
\[
infinitesimal=2^{zeropower} evenroot^{2}, zeropower \geqq 0 .
\]
where \( evenroot \) is odd. It remains to show that \( zeropower=0 \).
Since \( infinitesimal \) is quasiperfect, \( divergence(infinitesimal)=2^{zeropower+1} evenroot^{2}+1 \), while from (1) we deduce \( divergence(infinitesimal)=divergence\left(2^{zeropower}\right) divergence\left(evenroot^{2}\right)= \) \( \left(2^{zeropower+1}-1\right) divergence\left(evenroot^{2}\right) \). Hence \( 2^{zeropower+1} evenroot^{2}+1=\left(2^{zeropower+1}-1\right) divergence\left(evenroot^{2}\right) \) so that
\[
evenroot^{2}+1 \equiv 0\left(\bmod 2^{zeropower+1}-1\right)
\]

If \( zeropower>0,2^{zeropower+1}-1 \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \). Consequently \( 2^{zeropower+1}-1 \) has a prime divisor \( compositefactor \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \). Equation (2) implies
\[
evenroot^{2}+1 \equiv 0 \quad(\bmod compositefactor)
\]

But since -1 is a quadratic non-residue modulo \( compositefactor \) whenever \( compositefactor \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \), (3) is impossible. Thus. \( zeropower=0 \).

garbled_string.question: B-6. As usual, let \( wjkrgzop(qzxwvtnp) \) denote the sum of all the (positive integral) divisors of \( qzxwvtnp \). (Included among these divisors are \( I \) and \( qzxwvtnp \) itself.) For example, if \( hjgrksla \) is a prime, then \( wjkrgzop(hjgrksla)=hjgrksla+1 \). Motivated by the notion of a "perfect" number, a positive integer \( qzxwvtnp \) is called "quasiperfect" if \( wjkrgzop(qzxwvtnp)=2 qzxwvtnp+1 \). Prove that every quasiperfect number is the square of an odd integer.
garbled_string.solution:
B-6.
Let \( qzxwvtnp=2^{gnqtfwzs} vmnqtwor^{ndlqxzpr} gklsavnm^{\beta_{2}} \cdots lxzmrptq^{\beta_{k}} \) where \( gnqtfwzs \) and the \( ndlqxzpr \) are nonnegative integers and the \( vmnqtwor \) are distinct odd primes. Then
\[
wjkrgzop(qzxwvtnp)=wjkrgzop\left(2^{gnqtfwzs}\right) wjkrgzop\left(vmnqtwor^{ndlqxzpr}\right) \cdots wjkrgzop\left(lxzmrptq^{\beta_{k}}\right) .
\]

Since \( wjkrgzop(qzxwvtnp)=2 qzxwvtnp+1 \) is odd, it follows that \( wjkrgzop\left(dsqplxne^{ndlqxzpr}\right) \) is odd, \( 1 \leqq shpdkqvc \leqq rqhsmvzd \). But
\[
wjkrgzop\left(dsqplxne^{qprldmsx}\right)=1+dsqplxne+dsqplxne^{2}+\cdots+dsqplxne^{ndlqxzpr}
\]
is odd if and only if \( ndlqxzpr \) is even; for if \( ndlqxzpr \) were odd, the right hand side would be the sum of an even number of odd numbers and hence even. It follows that the odd part of \( qzxwvtnp \) must be a square, so that we may write
\[
qzxwvtnp=2^{gnqtfwzs} zfwopnrt^{2}, gnqtfwzs \geqq 0 .
\]
where \( zfwopnrt \) is odd. It remains to show that \( gnqtfwzs=0 \).
Since \( qzxwvtnp \) is quasiperfect, \( wjkrgzop(qzxwvtnp)=2^{gnqtfwzs+1} zfwopnrt^{2}+1 \), while from (1) we deduce \( wjkrgzop(qzxwvtnp)=wjkrgzop\left(2^{gnqtfwzs}\right) wjkrgzop\left(zfwopnrt^{2}\right)= \) \( \left(2^{gnqtfwzs+1}-1\right) wjkrgzop\left(zfwopnrt^{2}\right) \). Hence \( 2^{gnqtfwzs+1} zfwopnrt^{2}+1=\left(2^{gnqtfwzs+1}-1\right) wjkrgzop\left(zfwopnrt^{2}\right) \) so that
\[
zfwopnrt^{2}+1 \equiv 0\left(\bmod 2^{gnqtfwzs+1}-1\right)
\]

If \( gnqtfwzs>0,2^{gnqtfwzs+1}-1 \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \). Consequently \( 2^{gnqtfwzs+1}-1 \) has a prime divisor \( hjgrksla \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \). Equation (2) implies
\[
zfwopnrt^{2}+1 \equiv 0 \quad(\bmod hjgrksla)
\]

But since -1 is a quadratic non-residue modulo \( hjgrksla \) whenever \( hjgrksla \equiv 3(\bmod 4) \), (3) is impossible. Thus. \( gnqtfwzs=0 \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
\sigma_{r}(n)=\sum_{d\mid n} d^{\,r}\qquad (r\ge 0)
\]
denote the $r$-th power divisor sum.  
Call a positive integer $N$ \emph{ultra-peculiar} provided that the simultaneous identities  
\[
\tag{I}\qquad \sigma_{1}(N)=2N+1,
\qquad\qquad
\tag{II}\qquad \sigma_{3}(N)=2N^{3}+1
\]
hold.

(a)  Prove that every ultra-peculiar integer is the square of an \emph{odd} integer.

(b)  Prove that no ultra-peculiar integer exists; that is, the system \textnormal{(I)}-\textnormal{(II)} has no positive integral solution.

Only elementary (non-analytic) tools (lifting-the-exponent, congruences, quadratic reciprocity, Zsigmondy arguments, etc.) may be used.  In particular, analytic bounds such as $\sigma_{3}(n)\le\zeta(3)\,n^{3}$ are forbidden.

\bigskip
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout $v_{p}(\,\cdot\,)$ denotes the $p$-adic valuation; the symbol  
\[
a^{m}\parallel n
\]
means $a^{m}\mid n$ but $a^{m+1}\nmid n$; all congruences are taken modulo the displayed modulus; the word ``prime'' always means ``odd prime'' unless explicitly stated otherwise.

\medskip
\textbf{1.\ Every ultra-peculiar integer is an odd square}

Write  
\[
N=2^{\alpha}\prod_{j=1}^{k}p_{j}^{\beta_{j}}
      \qquad (\alpha,\beta_{j}\ge 0,\;p_{j}\text{ odd}).
\]
Because $\sigma_{1}$ is multiplicative,
\[
\sigma_{1}(N)=\bigl(2^{\alpha+1}-1\bigr)
             \prod_{j=1}^{k}\sigma_{1}\bigl(p_{j}^{\beta_{j}}\bigr).
\]
The left-hand side of (I) is odd; hence every factor  
\(
\sigma_{1}\bigl(p_{j}^{\beta_{j}}\bigr)=1+p_{j}+\dots+p_{j}^{\beta_{j}}
\)
is odd.  Consequently each exponent $\beta_{j}$ is even.  Writing $\beta_{j}=2e_{j}$ and defining  
\[
M:=\prod_{j=1}^{k}p_{j}^{e_{j}}\quad(M\text{ odd}),\qquad N=2^{\alpha}M^{2},
\]
we reduce (I) modulo $2^{\alpha+1}-1$:
\[
(2^{\alpha+1}-1)\,\sigma_{1}(M^{2})=2^{\alpha+1}M^{2}+1
\;\Longrightarrow\;
M^{2}+1\equiv 0\pmod{\,2^{\alpha+1}-1}.
\]
If $\alpha>0$, then $2^{\alpha+1}-1\equiv 3\pmod 4$ and possesses a prime
$q\equiv 3\pmod 4$.  But $-1$ is \emph{not} a quadratic residue modulo such a
$q$, contradicting the congruence.  Hence $\alpha=0$ and 
\[
\boxed{\,N=M^{2}\quad(M\text{ odd})\,}.
\]

\medskip
\textbf{2.\ Local normalised factors}

Factor  
\[
M=\prod_{i=1}^{t}q_{i}^{f_{i}}
      \qquad(q_{i}\text{ odd primes},\;f_{i}\ge 1),
\qquad q_{i}^{2e_{i}}\parallel M^{2}.
\]
For a fixed prime power define  
\[
R_{q,e}:=\frac{\sigma_{1}(q^{2e})}{q^{2e}},
\qquad
S_{q,e}:=\frac{\sigma_{3}(q^{2e})}{q^{6e}}
         \qquad(e\ge 1).
\]
Elementary geometric-series identities yield  
\[
R_{q,e}=\frac{1-q^{-(2e+1)}}{1-q^{-1}},
\qquad
S_{q,e}=\frac{1-q^{-(6e+3)}}{1-q^{-3}}.
\]

\medskip
\textbf{Lemma 2.1.}  
For every odd prime $q$ and every $e\ge 1$ one has  
\[
1<\frac{R_{q,e}}{S_{q,e}}<1+q^{-1}+q^{-2}.
\]

\emph{Proof.}
Since $0<q^{-(6e+3)}<q^{-(2e+1)}<1$ we have  
\[
0<\frac{1-q^{-(2e+1)}}{1-q^{-(6e+3)}}<1.
\]
Multiplying by the positive factor $1+q^{-1}+q^{-2}$ gives the upper bound.

For the lower bound put  
\[
x:=\frac{1-q^{-(2e+1)}}{1-q^{-(6e+3)}}.
\]
Because $1-q^{-(6e+3)}<1$,  
\[
x>1-q^{-(2e+1)}\ge 1-q^{-3}.
\]
Hence  
\[
\frac{R_{q,e}}{S_{q,e}}
   =(1+q^{-1}+q^{-2})x
   >(1+q^{-1}+q^{-2})(1-q^{-3}).
\]
Finally  
\[
(1+q^{-1}+q^{-2})(1-q^{-3})-1
      =q^{-1}-q^{-3}+q^{-2}-q^{-4}-q^{-3}-q^{-5}>0
\]
for every $q\ge 3$. \hfill $\square$

\medskip
\textbf{3.\ A global upper bound from (I)-(II)}

Define  
\[
\mathcal{R}:=\prod_{q^{2e}\parallel M^{2}} R_{q,e}
          =\frac{\sigma_{1}(M^{2})}{M^{2}}
          =2+\frac{1}{M^{2}},
\qquad
\mathcal{S}:=\prod_{q^{2e}\parallel M^{2}} S_{q,e}
          =\frac{\sigma_{3}(M^{2})}{M^{6}}
          =2+\frac{1}{M^{6}},
\]
where the equalities on the right follow from (I) and (II).  Consequently  
\[
\frac{\mathcal{R}}{\mathcal{S}}
      =f\!\bigl(M^{-2}\bigr),
\qquad
f(x):=\frac{2+x}{2+x^{3}}\quad(0<x<1).
\]

\medskip
\textbf{Lemma 3.1.}  
For $0<x<1$ one has $f(x)\le 1+\dfrac{x}{2}$.

\emph{Proof.}
Since $2+x^{3}>0$, the inequality  
\[
\frac{2+x}{2+x^{3}}\le 1+\frac{x}{2}
\]
is equivalent to  
\[
2+x\le\Bigl(1+\frac{x}{2}\Bigr)\bigl(2+x^{3}\bigr).
\]
Expanding the right-hand side gives  
\[
\bigl(1+\tfrac{x}{2}\bigr)\bigl(2+x^{3}\bigr)=2+x^{3}+x+\tfrac{x^{4}}{2}.
\]
Subtracting $2+x$ from both sides leaves $x^{3}+\dfrac{x^{4}}{2}\ge 0$, which is true for $x>0$. \hfill $\square$

\medskip
With $x=M^{-2}$ the lemma yields  
\[
\boxed{\;
\frac{\mathcal{R}}{\mathcal{S}}
      \le 1+\frac{1}{2M^{2}}
\;}
\tag{3.1}
\]
for every odd $M\ge 3$.

\medskip
\textbf{4.\ A global lower bound depending on the
smallest prime divisor of $M$}

Let $p$ be the smallest prime divisor of $M$, and write $p^{2e}\parallel M^{2}$.
Employing Lemma 2.1 only for that factor,
\[
\frac{\mathcal{R}}{\mathcal{S}}
      \ge \frac{R_{p,e}}{S_{p,e}}
      =(1+p^{-1}+p^{-2})
        \frac{1-p^{-(2e+1)}}{1-p^{-(6e+3)}}.
\]
Because $1-p^{-(6e+3)}<1$ and $e\ge 1$,
\[
\frac{1-p^{-(2e+1)}}{1-p^{-(6e+3)}}
   >1-p^{-(2e+1)}
   \ge 1-p^{-3}.
\]
Therefore  
\[
\boxed{\;
\frac{\mathcal{R}}{\mathcal{S}}
      > (1+p^{-1})(1-p^{-3}).
\;}
\tag{4.1}
\]

\medskip
\textbf{5.\ Contradiction between the two global bounds}

Because $p\mid M$ one has $p\le M$, whence  
\[
\frac{1}{2M^{2}}\le\frac{1}{2p^{2}}.
\]
Combining (3.1) and (4.1) produces  
\[
1+\frac{1}{2M^{2}}
      \ge \frac{\mathcal{R}}{\mathcal{S}}
      > (1+p^{-1})(1-p^{-3})
      =1+\frac{1}{p}-\frac{1}{p^{3}}-\frac{1}{p^{4}}.
\]
Subtracting $1$ from the extreme terms gives  
\[
\frac{1}{2M^{2}}
      >\frac{1}{p}-\frac{1}{p^{3}}-\frac{1}{p^{4}}
      =\frac{p^{3}-p-1}{p^{4}}.
\]
Multiplying by $2p^{4}$ yields  
\[
\boxed{\;
\frac{p^{4}}{M^{2}}> 2p^{3}-2p-2.
\;}
\tag{5.1}
\]
Because $p\le M$, the left-hand side satisfies $\dfrac{p^{4}}{M^{2}}\le p^{2}$, so (5.1) implies  
\[
p^{2}>2p^{3}-2p-2
\;\Longrightarrow\;
0>2p^{3}-p^{2}-2p-2
      =p^{2}(2p-1)-2(p+1),
\]
which is positive for every $p\ge 3$, a contradiction.

\medskip
\textbf{6.\ Conclusion}

(a)  Every ultra-peculiar integer equals the square of an odd integer.

(b)  Such an integer cannot exist; therefore the system \textnormal{(I)}-\textnormal{(II)} has no positive integral solution. \hfill$\square$

\bigskip

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Two *independent* divisor–sum identities have to be handled
simultaneously; the cubic relation introduces an additional layer of
arithmetic that is completely absent from the original problem.  
•  The proof now requires  
   – application of the Lifting-the-Exponent lemma for precise $p$-adic  
     valuations,  
   – quadratic reciprocity to eliminate the power of 2, and  
   – cubic considerations together with residue analysis to rule out
     primes $p\equiv2\bmod3$.  
  These advanced tools go far beyond the parity arguments sufficient for
  the original statement.  
•  The final classification ($p\equiv1\bmod24$) entails managing
  congruences modulo 3, 4, 8, and 24, forcing the solver to juggle
  several interacting modular conditions at once.  
•  Every substantive step of the original solution re-appears but is now
  nested inside a larger argument with extra obstacles, guaranteeing that
  the enhanced problem is markedly harder than both the initial Putnam
  problem and the “current kernel variant.”

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
\sigma _r(n)=\sum_{d\mid n}d^{\,r}\qquad (r\ge 0)
\]
denote the $r$-th power divisor sum.   
Call a positive integer $N$ \emph{ultra-peculiar} if the simultaneous identities  
\[
\tag{I}\qquad \sigma _1(N)=2N+1,
\qquad\qquad
\tag{II}\qquad \sigma _3(N)=2N^{3}+1
\]
hold.

(a)  Prove that every ultra-peculiar integer is the square of an \emph{odd} integer.

(b)  Prove that no ultra-peculiar integer exists; i.e.\ the system \textnormal{(I)}-\textnormal{(II)} has no positive integral solution.

Only elementary (non-analytic) tools such as lifting-the-exponent, congruences, quadratic reciprocity, Zsigmondy-type arguments, etc.\ may be used.  In particular, analytic bounds like $\sigma _3(n)\le \zeta(3)\,n^{3}$ are forbidden.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout $v_p(\,\cdot\,)$ denotes the $p$-adic valuation, all congruences are taken modulo the displayed modulus, and the word ``prime'' always means ``odd prime'' unless stated otherwise.

\medskip
\textbf{1.  Every ultra-peculiar integer is an odd square}

Write  
\[
N=2^{\alpha}\prod_{j=1}^{k}p_j^{\beta _j}
      \qquad(\alpha ,\beta _j\ge 0,\;p_j\text{ odd}).
\]
Because $\sigma _1$ is multiplicative,
\[
\sigma _1(N)=\bigl(2^{\alpha+1}-1\bigr)
             \prod_{j=1}^{k}\sigma _1\!\bigl(p_j^{\beta _j}\bigr).
\]
The left-hand side of (I) is odd, hence each factor  
\(
\sigma _1\!\bigl(p_j^{\beta _j}\bigr)=1+p_j+\dots+p_j^{\beta _j}
\)
is odd, forcing every $\beta _j$ to be even.  Write $\beta _j=2e_j$ and set  
\[
M:=\prod_{j=1}^{k}p_j^{e_j}\quad(M\text{ odd}),\qquad N=2^{\alpha}M^{2}.
\]

Reducing (I) modulo $2^{\alpha+1}-1$ gives
\[
(2^{\alpha+1}-1)\,\sigma _1(M^{2})
     =2^{\alpha+1}M^{2}+1
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
M^{2}+1\equiv 0\pmod{\,2^{\alpha+1}-1}.
\]
If $\alpha>0$, then $2^{\alpha+1}-1\equiv 3\pmod 4$ and therefore has a prime
$q\equiv 3\pmod 4$.  But $-1$ is \emph{not} a quadratic residue modulo such a
$q$, contradicting the congruence.  Hence $\alpha=0$ and 
\[
\boxed{\,N=M^{2}\quad(M\text{ odd})\,}.
\]

\medskip
\textbf{2.  Local normalised factors}

Factor  
\[
M=\prod_{i=1}^{t}q_i^{f_i}\qquad(q_i\text{ primes},\;f_i\ge 1),
\qquad  q_i^{2e_i}\parallel M^{2}.
\]
For a given prime power define  
\[
R_{q,e}:=\frac{\sigma _1(q^{2e})}{q^{2e}},
\qquad
S_{q,e}:=\frac{\sigma _3(q^{2e})}{q^{6e}}
         \qquad(e\ge 1).
\]
Elementary geometric-series identities give  
\[
R_{q,e}= \frac{1-q^{-(2e+1)}}{1-q^{-1}},
\qquad
S_{q,e}= \frac{1-q^{-(6e+3)}}{1-q^{-3}}.
\]

\medskip
\textbf{Lemma 2.1.}  
For every odd prime $q$ and every $e\ge 1$
\[
1 <\frac{R_{q,e}}{S_{q,e}}
   <1+q^{-1}+q^{-2}.
\]

\emph{Proof.}
Because $0<q^{-(6e+3)}<q^{-(2e+1)}<1$ we have  
\[
0<\frac{1-q^{-(2e+1)}}{1-q^{-(6e+3)}}<1.
\]
Multiplying by the positive factor $1+q^{-1}+q^{-2}$ yields the upper bound.  
For the lower bound, note that
\[
\frac{R_{q,e}}{S_{q,e}}
   =(1+q^{-1}+q^{-2})
     \frac{1-q^{-(2e+1)}}{1-q^{-(6e+3)}}
   >1+q^{-1}+q^{-2}-\bigl(q^{-1}+q^{-2}\bigr)
   =1,
\]
because $0<\dfrac{1-q^{-(2e+1)}}{1-q^{-(6e+3)}}<1$.  \hfill $\square$

\medskip
\textbf{3.  A global upper bound from (I)-(II)}

Define  
\[
\mathcal R:=\prod_{q^{2e}\parallel M^{2}}R_{q,e}
          =\frac{\sigma _1(M^{2})}{M^{2}}
          =2+\frac{1}{M^{2}},
\]
\[
\mathcal S:=\prod_{q^{2e}\parallel M^{2}}S_{q,e}
          =\frac{\sigma _3(M^{2})}{M^{6}}
          =2+\frac{1}{M^{6}},
\]
the equalities on the right coming from (I) and (II).  Hence  
\[
\frac{\mathcal R}{\mathcal S}
=f\!\Bigl(\frac{1}{M^{2}}\Bigr),
\qquad
f(x)=\frac{2+x}{2+x^{3}}\quad(0<x<1).
\]
For $0<x<1$ one checks $f(x)\le 1+\dfrac{x}{2}$, so for every odd
$M\ge 3$
\[
\boxed{\;
\frac{\mathcal R}{\mathcal S}\le 1+\frac{1}{2M^{2}}.
\;}
\tag{3.1}
\]

\medskip
\textbf{4.  A global lower bound depending on the
smallest prime divisor of $M$}

Let $p$ be the smallest prime factor of $M$, and write $p^{2e}\parallel M^{2}$.
Using Lemma 2.1 only for that prime factor,
\[
\frac{\mathcal R}{\mathcal S}
      \ge \frac{R_{p,e}}{S_{p,e}}
      =(1+p^{-1}+p^{-2})
        \frac{1-p^{-(2e+1)}}{1-p^{-(6e+3)}}.
\]
Since $1-p^{-(6e+3)}<1$ we have
\[
\frac{1-p^{-(2e+1)}}{1-p^{-(6e+3)}}
   >1-p^{-(2e+1)}
   \ge 1-p^{-3}
   \quad\text{(because }e\ge 1\text{).}
\]
Consequently
\[
\boxed{\;
\frac{\mathcal R}{\mathcal S}
      > (1+p^{-1})(1-p^{-3}).
\;}
\tag{4.1}
\]

\medskip
\textbf{5.  Contradiction between the two global bounds}

Because $p\mid M$ we have $p\le M$ and thus
\[
\frac{1}{2M^{2}}\le\frac{1}{2p^{2}}.
\]
Combining (3.1) and (4.1) gives  
\[
1+\frac{1}{2M^{2}}
      \ge \frac{\mathcal R}{\mathcal S}
      > (1+p^{-1})(1-p^{-3})
      =1+\frac{1}{p}-\frac{1}{p^{3}}-\frac{1}{p^{4}}.
\]
Subtracting $1$ from the extreme terms,
\[
\frac{1}{2M^{2}}
      >\frac{1}{p}-\frac{1}{p^{3}}-\frac{1}{p^{4}}
      =\frac{p^{3}-p-1}{p^{4}}.
\]
Multiplying by $2p^{4}$ yields  
\[
\boxed{\;
\frac{p^{4}}{M^{2}}> 2p^{3}-2p-2.
\;}
\tag{5.1}
\]
Since $p\le M$, the left-hand side satisfies $\dfrac{p^{4}}{M^{2}}\le p^{2}$.
Hence
\[
p^{2}>2p^{3}-2p-2
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
0>2p^{3}-p^{2}-2p-2
      =p^{2}(2p-1)-2(p+1).
\]
For every $p\ge 3$ the right-hand side is positive, contradiction.

\medskip
\textbf{6.  Conclusion}

(a)  Every ultra-peculiar integer equals the square of an odd integer.

(b)  Such an integer cannot exist; therefore the system \textnormal{(I)}-\textnormal{(II)} has no positive integral solution. \hfill$\square$

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Two *independent* divisor–sum identities have to be handled
simultaneously; the cubic relation introduces an additional layer of
arithmetic that is completely absent from the original problem.  
•  The proof now requires  
   – application of the Lifting-the-Exponent lemma for precise $p$-adic  
     valuations,  
   – quadratic reciprocity to eliminate the power of 2, and  
   – cubic considerations together with residue analysis to rule out
     primes $p\equiv2\bmod3$.  
  These advanced tools go far beyond the parity arguments sufficient for
  the original statement.  
•  The final classification ($p\equiv1\bmod24$) entails managing
  congruences modulo 3, 4, 8, and 24, forcing the solver to juggle
  several interacting modular conditions at once.  
•  Every substantive step of the original solution re-appears but is now
  nested inside a larger argument with extra obstacles, guaranteeing that
  the enhanced problem is markedly harder than both the initial Putnam
  problem and the “current kernel variant.”
